name: Median, Mode, Range and More Charts
whatIsIt: Beyond the mean (average), data has more measures. MEDIAN is the MIDDLE value when the numbers are put in order. MODE is the MOST COMMON value. RANGE is the highest minus the lowest — how spread out the data is. Different charts suit different data: BAR graphs compare categories, LINE graphs show change over time, PIE charts show parts of a whole.
whyNeeded: The average alone can mislead. Median resists odd extreme values, mode finds the most popular choice, and range shows the spread. Choosing the right chart makes data honest and clear — a key skill for news, science and decisions.
realLifeUses: Median house price (fairer than mean when one is huge)
The most common shoe size to stock (mode)
Temperature change through the day (line graph)
How a budget splits into parts (pie chart)

vocabulary.meaning: The middle value when the data is put in order.
The value that appears most often.
Highest value minus lowest value — the spread.

story.title: The Fair Middle
story.text: Ira's five test scores are 6, 7, 7, 8, 9. Coach asks for the median. She orders them (already in order) and picks the middle: 7. 'And the mode?' '7 too — it appears most.' 'The range?' '9 − 6 = 3.' Coach nods: 'Three numbers, three different stories about the same data.'
story.extractedProblem: Scores 6, 7, 7, 8, 9 — what is the median (middle value)?
story.answerInStory: 7 — the middle number when they're in order.

visual.caption: Scores 6,7,7,8,9: the tallest bar (7) is the MODE; the middle value in order (7) is the MEDIAN; range = 9 − 6 = 3.

teachingGallery.title: Middle, most, spread
teachingGallery.note: Order the data; middle = median, tallest = mode, top − bottom = range.

examples.caption: Temperatures over days → line graph fits change over time.
Pie chart: a slice = a part of the whole.

standardMethod.summary: Order the data. Median = middle value; mode = most frequent; range = highest − lowest. Pick the chart that fits the data type.
steps: Put the numbers in order (smallest to largest).
MEDIAN: the middle value (for an even count, average the two middle ones).
MODE: the value that appears most often; RANGE: highest − lowest.
Chart: bars compare categories, lines show change over time, pies show parts of a whole.

workedExamples.problem: Find the median and range of 3, 8, 5, 4, 9.

commonMistakes.mistake: Taking the middle of the list WITHOUT ordering the numbers first.
commonMistakes.fix: Always put the data in order before picking the middle — the median needs sorted values.

commonMistakes.mistake: Giving the highest value as the range instead of highest − lowest.
commonMistakes.fix: Range is the SPREAD: highest value minus lowest value.

challenge.q: Which chart best shows temperature CHANGE over a week?

questions.q: Before finding the median you must…

teachBackPrompt: Collect a small set of numbers at home (ages, or scores in a game). Show a grown-up how to find the median (order, take the middle), the mode (most common) and the range (highest − lowest) — and say which chart would show it best.

revisionCard.summary: Order the data first. MEDIAN = middle value (average the two middle if even count). MODE = most frequent. RANGE = highest − lowest (spread). Charts: bars compare categories, lines show change over time, pies show parts of a whole.

meta.curriculum: CBSE/NCERT Class 7-8 (statistics — median, mode, range, charts)

rememberIt.hook: Mean = average, Median = middle, Mode = most
rememberIt.unpack: Mean = sum / count. Median = middle value when sorted. Mode = the value that appears most. 'Median = Middle', 'Mode = Most'.

studentQuestions.a: Mean for balanced data; median when there are extreme outliers (it is not dragged by them); mode for the most common category.

studentQuestions.q: How do I find the median of an even count?
studentQuestions.a: Sort the values, then average the two middle ones.

formulas.name: Median
formulas.remember: Even count -> average the two middles
